---
title: "Multivariate - PCA"
format: html
---

# Learning objectives  
Our learning objectives are to:  

  - Explore what is and when to use multivariate analysis  
  - Explore different types of multivariate analysis  
  - Import our feature-engineered weather data set to be analyzed  
  - Understand what is multicollinearity, its effects, implications, and remedies   
  - Run principal component analysis (PCA) for dimensionality reduction  

# Introduction  
## Terminology  

In the following general equation:  

    y ~ x

Y is normally referred to as:  
  - Response variable  
  - Dependent variable  
  - Predicted variable  

X is normally referred to as:  
  - Explanatory variable  
  - Independent variable  
  - Predictor variable  

## What is multivariate analysis?  

Most common: multiple Xs (predictors)  

               y ~ x1 + x2 + .... xn  
               yield ~ precip + temp + som + hybrid  
    
Perhaps multiple Xs (predictors) without a Y (outcome)  

              ~ x1 + x2 + .... xn

While some can have multiple Ys (outcomes)  

               y1 + y2 ~ x1 + x2 (MANOVA)  
               yield + biomass ~ precip + temp  
  
## When to use multivariate?  
When multiple predictors can be used to explain an outcome.  

When multiple predictors can be used to create data-driven groups (clusters).  

Commonly not a "designed experiment" analysis, but an exploratory relationship approach.  

## Main types of multivariate  
- Dimensionality reduction  
    - **Principal component analysis (PCA)**  
    - Principal coordinate analysis (PCoA)  
    - Factor analysis  
    
- Clustering 
    - **k-means**, c-means, x-means  
    - Non-numerical multidimensional scaling  
    - nearest neighbor  
  
- Relationship between variables / Prediction  
  - Multiple linear regression/stepwise regression  
  - **Random forest**    
  - **Conditional inference tree**  

## A few ways to characterize multivariate analysis  
- Based on analysis goal:  

  - Decrease number of dimensions (e.g. PCA)
  - Create clusters  (e.g. k-means)
  - Establish significant relationships among multiple predictors and an outcome (e.g. random forest)  

- Based on Y (outcome) existence:
    - Supervised  (i.e. Random Forest. do you have a y variable in your data? if yes, you have a supervised algorithm. Youre saying what youre looking for, just "help me explain how to get it". i.e. if you give it the site columns as y variable and then give it all the weather variables. Use my x variables (weather measurements) to give me this output.)
    - Unsupervised  (Clustering Algorithm - if you do not have a y-variable. You dont know what the output is, help me look at it based on this data. i.e. you can give the algorithm everything as asking it to group it based on similarities in the data.)

- If Y exists, based on its type:  
    - Categorical: classification  
    - Numerical: regression  


imagine our strength variable is our numerical variable in a random forest, this would be a regression. 
However we might want to simplify by transforming our numerical variable and transforming it to a categorical variable  - i.e. by making quantiles (low, medium, high strength) - then this model becomes a classification. 

Random forest can be either classification or regression depending on what the y-variable is. 

# Setup  
```{r}
#| message: false
#| warning: false

#install.packages("ggcorrplot")
#install.packages("factoextra")
#install.packages("ggpmisc")

library(tidyverse)
library(ggcorrplot)
library(broom)
library(car)
library(factoextra) #explore PCA results
library(ggpmisc)
```

```{r}
weather <- read_csv("../Data/weather_monthsum.csv")

weather
```


# EDA  
How are variables related? Any strong correlations that we should watch out for?  

Let's create a correlation matrix
```{r correlation matrix p-values}
# Estimating significance matrix
p.mat <- weather %>%
  dplyr::select(-year,-site) %>%
  cor_pmat()

p.mat
```

Plotting  
```{r correlation matrix plot}
weather %>%
  dplyr::select(-year, -site) %>%
  cor() %>%
  ggcorrplot(hc.order = TRUE, 
             digits = 1,
             type = "lower", 
             p.mat = p.mat, 
             sig.level = 0.05,
             insig = "blank",
             lab = TRUE)

ggsave("../output/corrmat.png",
       height = 25,
       width = 22,
       bg = "white")
```

A bit difficult to see because we have so many predictors, let's extract in table format.  

```{r highest correlations}
weather %>%
  dplyr::select(-year, -site) %>%
  cor() %>% # calculates r (-1 to 1)
  as.data.frame() %>%
  rownames_to_column() %>%
  pivot_longer(cols = -rowname) %>%
  filter(abs(value) > .85 & value != 1) %>%
  arrange(desc(value)) %>%
  distinct(value, .keep_all = T)

```

```{r lowest correlations}
weather %>%
  dplyr::select(-year, -site) %>%
  cor() %>% # calculates r (-1 to 1)
  as.data.frame() %>%
  rownames_to_column() %>%
  pivot_longer(cols = -rowname) %>%
  filter(abs(value) < .05 & value != 1) %>%
  arrange(abs(value)) %>%
  distinct(value, .keep_all = T)

```

How do variables relate to fiber strength in a bivariate relationship?  

```{r r2}
weather %>%
  dplyr::select(-site, -year) %>%
  pivot_longer(cols=-strength_gtex) %>%
  group_by(name) %>%
  nest() %>%
  mutate(r2 = map_dbl(data,
                  ~lm(strength_gtex ~ value,
                      data = .x) %>%
                    glance(.) %>%
                    pull(r.squared)
                  )) %>%
  arrange(desc(r2))


```

```{r r2 plot}
weather %>%
  dplyr::select(-site, -year) %>%
  pivot_longer(cols=-strength_gtex) %>%
  group_by(name) %>%
  nest() %>%
  mutate(r2 = map_dbl(data,
                  ~lm(strength_gtex ~ value,
                      data = .x) %>%
                    glance(.) %>%
                    pull(r.squared)
                  )) %>%
  arrange(desc(r2)) %>%
  ungroup() %>%
  slice(1:6) %>% # top 6 values
  unnest(data) %>% #take out the tibbles, open into the main dataframe
  ggplot(aes(x = value, 
             y = strength_gtex))+
  geom_point(shape = 21, 
             alpha = .7, 
             fill = "purple")+
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", 
              se = F, 
              color = "black", 
              size = 1)+
  facet_wrap(~name, 
             scales = "free_x", 
             ncol=2) 

```

# Multicollinearity 
## Concepts  
Multicollinearity definition: more than two explanatory variables in a multiple regression model are highly linearly related.  


Multicollinearity is an issue because:  

- Model estimates magnitude and direction (+ or -) can change for multicollinear variables compared to a non-multicollinear model.  
  
- Model estimates standard error are inflated, directly affecting p-values, estimate significance, and power.  
  

## Applied example  
Let's select a few variables to run some tests.  
Two uncorrelated variables:
```{r uncorrelated}
weather %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = mean_dayl.s_Apr, 
             y = sum_prcp.mm_Oct)) +
  geom_point() +
  geom_smooth(method="lm") #+
  #stat_correlation() #this just adds the r^2 to the plot, but its causing issues
```

Two correlated variables:
```{r correlated}
weather %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = mean_dayl.s_Apr, 
             y = mean_dayl.s_May))+
  geom_point() +
  geom_smooth(method="lm")

```

Now let's fit some models with one or two uncorrelated and correlated variables explaining fiberstrength and see what happens.  

```{r strength ~ mean_dayl.s_Apr}
lm_dayl.apr <- lm(strength_gtex ~ mean_dayl.s_Apr, #strength explained by mean day length in April, simple linear model. Then below data = weather where weather is our dataframe
                  data = weather) %>%
  tidy()  %>%  #givrs as a dataframe thats easier to change
  filter(term != "(Intercept)") %>%
  mutate(mod = "lm_dayl.apr") #model name
  
lm_dayl.apr

```

```{r strength ~ sum_prcp.mm_Oct}
lm_prp.oct <- lm(strength_gtex ~ sum_prcp.mm_Oct,
   data = weather) %>%
  tidy() %>%
  filter(term!="(Intercept)") %>%
  mutate(mod="lm_prp.oct")

lm_prp.oct
```

```{r strength ~ mean_dayl.s_Apr + sum_prcp.mm_Oct}
lm_dayl.prcp <- lm(strength_gtex ~ mean_dayl.s_Apr + 
                   sum_prcp.mm_Oct, #strength explained by April day length AND Oct precipitation
   data = weather) %>%
  tidy() %>%
  filter(term != "(Intercept)") %>%
  mutate(mod = "lm_2var.uncor") #gave both entries the same name at the end

lm_dayl.prcp

```

```{r strength ~ mean_dayl.s_Apr + mean_dayl.s_May}
lm_daylapr.daylmay <- lm(strength_gtex ~ mean_dayl.s_Apr + 
                  mean_dayl.s_May,
   data = weather) %>%
  tidy() %>%
  filter(term!="(Intercept)") %>%
  mutate(mod="lm_2var.cor")

lm_daylapr.daylmay

```

```{r Checking multicollinearity}
lm_dayl.apr %>%
  bind_rows(lm_prp.oct, 
            lm_dayl.prcp, #THESE ARE THE OBJECT NAMES OF THE MODELS FROM ABOVE
            lm_daylapr.daylmay) %>%
  mutate(mod=factor(mod,
                    levels=c("lm_dayl.apr", #in the column mod, you are ranking these levels/rows manually here. Rearranging here so that when you plot it it follows this order
                             "lm_prp.oct",
                             "lm_2var.uncor",
                             "lm_2var.cor"))) %>%
  filter(term != "mean_dayl.s_May") %>% #removing the term mean day length in may, which is coming from the last model we ran. Taking it out bc its not in our other models. We just want to look at April for now. 
  ggplot(aes(x=mod)) +
  geom_pointrange(aes(y = estimate, #the value of the y estimates of the above model tibbles
                      ymin = estimate - std.error, #these also come from the tibbles above. This can be the confidence intervals, it can be anything. Provide a POINT, and then a RANGE AROUND. 
                      ymax = estimate + std.error
                      )) +
  facet_wrap(~term, scales = "free_y") + #just allowing the y scale to be free, not the x
  scale_x_discrete(guide = guide_axis(n.dodge = 2)) #discrete because our x axis labels come from what are discrete variables, and then dodge/shift every other so there is less crowding on the axis



#the two that are at the same points suggest that mean day length being in a model are one that was highly correlated and one that we knew was uncorrelated. Adding an uncorrelated variable did not impact Mean day length's ability to predict strength
#my contrast, when mean day length of april is with another highly correlated model, the slope is different and less correlated.


#When Sum Precip October was alone, it did not differ from how it was with an uncorrelated model.
```

What has happened with **mean_dayl.s_April** and **sum_prcp.mm_Oct** estimates and standard error when modeled i) alone, or with another uncorrelated variable vs. ii) with another correlated variable?

One way to check the degree of multicollinearity between two predictos is with the *variance inflation factor (VIF)*.  

VIF values range from 1 to positive infinite.  
General rule of thumb:  

- VIF ~ 1: no multicollinearity  
- VIF between 1 and 5: moderate multicollinearity  
- VIF > 5: severe multicollinearity  

Let's check the variance inflation factor (VIF) of both uncorrelated and correlated models
```{r vif uncorrelated}
# Uncorrelated

vif(lm(strength_gtex ~ mean_dayl.s_Apr + 
                   sum_prcp.mm_Oct, #strength explained by April day length AND Oct precipitation
   data = weather))
```


```{r vif correlated}
# Correlated. This is the one that we know we have multicolinearity. The result is a higher value, showing that this mdoel has problems. Adding in mean day length and april and may together significantly changed the slope, value, etc. 
lm(strength_gtex ~ mean_dayl.s_Apr + 
                  mean_dayl.s_May,
   data = weather) %>%
  vif()


#you can either put the model into vif() or write the model then pipe into vif() at the end
```

## Dealing with multicollinearity  
So what now?  
How can we deal with correlated variables in a multivariate approach?  
Options:  

- *Dimensionality reduction*
    - Reducing the number of x variables in the model. Maybe we go into the literature and select two that are known to be looked into. Give only the variables that we know are impactful, or do math with principle component analysis.
    - By hand (selecting predictors based on previous knowledge, literature, etc.)  
    - By math  
  
- *Algorithm that handles multicollinearity behind the scenes*
    - Variable selection, the model itself selects variables for you instead of using all 72 variables in the model 
    - Multivariate by ensembling multiple bivariates. Training machine learning models to look at each thing one by one/individually

**Many multivariate approaches deal with some sort of similarity/dissimilarity measure among predictors.**

In those cases, predictors with vastly different scales (e.g. mean_tmin.c_Jan from -10 to 10 C vs mean_dayl.s_Jul from 48000 to 51000) need to be normalized so measurement scale does not affect variable importance.    

Thus, our numerical predictor variables need to be normalized (center and scale) before starting our multivariate analysis.  

Some analysis do the normalization for you (like PCA), and others don't (like k-means), so need to be aware of this to ensure data is normalized.  

Since both PCA and k-means only take numerical variables, let's select them now.  
```{r selecting only numerical vars}
weather_n <- weather %>% dplyr::select(-year, -site, - strength_gtex)
#pull the weather object. We want to take out the y variables and only put in the weather variables
weather_n
```

# PCA  
Principal component analysis (PCA) is a dimensionality reduction approach that accomodates only numerical variables.  

Finds linear relationships among predictors that can be represented in a lower number of uncorrelated dimensions.  

Works well when at least some predictors are correlated.  

PCA:  

- Is used for dimensionality reduction  
- Is an unsupervised analysis (no outcome)  
- Only takes predictors  
- Predictors need to be numerical  

Some PCA properties:  

- The number of PCs calculated is the same as the number of variables in the data set (e.g., in our case, 72).  

- The overall variance explained by each PC is greatest for PC1 and decreases as PC number increases (e.g., PC72 will explain the least variance).  

- All PCs are **orthogonal** (i.e., independent of each other).  

- The goal is to select a small number of PCs that explain a minimum threshold of the total variance (e.g., 70%).  

```{r pca in action}
knitr::include_graphics("https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fit:1200/1*V4H3Cu8qGr_90WANKSO9BA.gif")
```
Let's run PCA for our weather data.  

```{r pca model}
mod_pca <- prcomp(weather_n, scale. = T)
summary(mod_pca)
```

## Choosing number of PCs  

**why do we use PCA? To reduce the number of dimensions and variables**
Based on scree plot (total variance):  
```{r pca checking number of components}
# Scree plot. Visualize individually how much variability is explained by each one of the components. Goal is not to select all 72, but just an adequate amount to do PCA. This is the best when we have some hghly correlated variables. If none of the principle components are explaining your data, then we would see all the bars around the same. These are the individual proportions.
fviz_eig(mod_pca,
         addlabels = T)

```
    
PCs 1 and 2 explain ~35% and ~26% (61%) of total variance.
This indicates that original variables were correlated (as we expected).   

If wanted to use enough PCs to explain 70% of total variance, how many would we need?  
```{r PCs to explain 70pct variance}
mod_pca %>%
  get_eig() %>%
  mutate(pc = 1:72) %>%
  ggplot(aes(x= pc,
             y=cumulative.variance.percent)) +
  geom_bar(stat="identity") + 
  geom_hline(yintercept = 70) #this will show us how many PC's we need to explain 70% of the variance in our data. its 4 (3 is just below)


#this one will give the cumulative proportion
```
    
We would need 4 PCs.  
Normally we wish to use 2-3 PCs, but 4 is certainly better than 72 original variables.  

## Inspecting PCs  
Let's inspect PC1 through the loadings (weights) of each variable towards it.  

What are the weights that each variable received in this PC?  
```{r PC1 weights}
mod_pca$rotation %>%
  as.data.frame() %>% #this will make it the actual names as a dataframe. All PC1s are a combination of all weather variables, but with ONE of the variables having the greatest WEIGHT. 
  rownames_to_column(var = "var") %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = reorder(var,desc(PC1)), 
             y = PC1))+
  geom_bar(stat = "identity", 
           aes(fill = PC1),  #THIS IS SO WE ONLY SEE PC1 BELOW
           show.legend = F)+
  scale_fill_gradient(low = "red", high = "blue")+
  theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, 
                                   hjust = 1))


#The winter months at the beginning are the most positive, and the ones with the most negtive correlation are day lengths in the summer months. Now we can see the weight of them to interpret what the PC means. We could explain this PC as explanation by season. 
```

Which variables contributed most to PC1, regardless of direction?  

```{r variables contributing to PC1}
fviz_contrib(mod_pca,
             choice = "var",
             axes = 1)
```
  
Let's check the eigenvectors for both PCs 1 and 2 variables:  

```{r pca variable contribution }
fviz_pca_var(mod_pca, 
             col.var = "contrib", # Color by contributions to the PC
             gradient.cols = c("#00AFBB", "#E7B800", "#FC4E07"),
             repel = TRUE,     # Avoid text overlapping
             select.var = list(contrib = 5)) #only the top 5
#Each arrow here is the eigenvector for a different variable for PC1 and 2. Those that are in opposite directions you may say they are opposititely correlated. The LONGER the vector is, the more important they are. 
```
  
The longer is the eigenvector for a given variable, the more important it is towards that PC.  

Let's plot PC1 vs PC2 scores, look for any groupings.  

```{r PC1 vs PC2}
fviz_pca_ind(mod_pca,
             label = F)  

#THE POINTS ARE PCA 1 AND 2 FOR EACH SITE YEAR    
```
  
Appears that some groups exist.  


What did we learn?  

- Some original variables strongly correlated  
- Need at least 4 PCs to cover ~70% of original variables variance  
- Most important variables in PC1 were related to day length in winter and summer months.  

What now?  
Let's add the first 4 PCs to our original dataset and run a regression versus grain yield.    

```{r pca scores}
# Extract first 4 PCs scores
pca_scores <- mod_pca$x %>%
  as.data.frame() %>%
  dplyr::select(PC1:PC4) #inly 4 instead of the original 72

pca_scores
```

```{r pca regression}
# Adding PCs 1-4 scores to original data set
weather_postpca <- weather %>%
  bind_cols(pca_scores)

# Regression of yield ~ PCs

lm_pca <- lm(strength_gtex ~ PC1 +PC2 + PC3 + PC4,
          data = weather_postpca)

# Summary  
summary(lm_pca)   #SHOWS THAT PC2 and 3 are the most significant
```


```{r pca regression}
# Checking VIF
vif(lm_pca)  #1 means there is no multicollinearity between that specific predictor variable and any of th eothr predictor variables in that regression model (the lm). These would be what you would expect - all 72 are orthogonal, independent, non-corralated so we would expect 1 for all of them. 
```

```{r pca regression plots}
# Plotting strength vs PC1
ggplot(weather_postpca,
       aes(x = PC1,
           y = strength_gtex)
       ) +
  geom_point() +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm")
  #this one has a pretty flat relationship
```


```{r pca regression plots}
# Plotting strength vs PC2
ggplot(weather_postpca, 
       aes(x = PC2, y = strength_gtex))+
  geom_point()+
  geom_smooth(method = "lm")
# positive relationship.
```

```{r pca regression plots}
# Plotting strength vs PC3
ggplot(weather_postpca, 
       aes(x = PC3, y = strength_gtex))+
  geom_point()+
  geom_smooth(method = "lm")

#Negative relationship
```

Only PCs 2 and 3 explained strength (look at which variables were most important to each PC for interpretation).  

As expected, PCs were not multicollinear (VIF=1).  

# Summary  
In this exercise, we covered:  

  - When multivariate analysis can be used  
  - How multicollinearity is an issue and what to do to fix it   
  - PCA for dimensionality reduction  
  - How to validate results from PCA
